sources.notes: Statement and constraints; each operation increments a subtree after choosing the root for that operation.
The official implementation confirms the postorder recurrence that clamps the maximum child summary to the node interval and adds positive child deficits.
Cross-checks the edge-cut interpretation of operations and the greedy choice near the maximum child value.
Provides the fixed-assignment positive-edge-difference formula and an exchange argument for the interval-clamped choice.

annotation.summary: Choose one initial value in each node's interval, then repeatedly increment operation-defined subtrees until all values are equal; minimize the final common value.
annotation.constraints: Up to 1e5 test cases, n <= 2e5 per test, sum of n <= 2e5, and 0 <= l_i <= r_i <= 1e9. A linear traversal is required and the answer needs 64-bit arithmetic.
annotation.core_idea: Root the tree arbitrarily at node 1. A non-global operation increments exactly one component obtained by deleting an edge, so for fixed initial values a, every edge difference is corrected independently and the minimum final value is a_1 + sum over children v of max(0, a_v - a_parent(v)). For interval choices, define G_u(z) as the minimum contribution of the parent edge and all edges inside subtree u when the parent value is z. Inductively G_u(z) = c_u + max(0, b_u - z). In postorder, let M_u = max({l_u} union {b_v : v is a child of u}), set b_u = min(r_u, M_u), and set c_u = sum over children v of (c_v + max(0, b_v - b_u)). Raising b_u toward the largest child summary cannot hurt: the parent-edge term grows by at most one per unit while at least one child deficit falls by one. Therefore the answer is b_1 + c_1.
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(n) memory per test case; O(sum n) over all test cases.
tricks: View every non-global operation as incrementing one side of a single edge cut.
For fixed node values, express the final level as the root value plus all positive child-to-parent edge differences.
Compress each subtree objective to one hinge-function breakpoint b_u and an unavoidable cost c_u.
Clamp the largest child breakpoint into [l_u, r_u] and accumulate only the remaining positive deficits.

solution_variants.name: postorder-clamped-breakpoint-dp
solution_variants.summary: Build an arbitrary rooted parent order and process it in reverse. Each node stores b_u, its interval-clamped maximum child breakpoint, and c_u, the sum of child costs plus max(0, b_v - b_u) over children. Output b_root + c_root.

tags.evidence: The solution represents every rooted subtree by the DP summary (b_u, c_u), equivalently the cost function G_u(z) = c_u + max(0, b_u - z), and combines child summaries in postorder.

At node u, choosing b_u as the interval-clamped maximum child summary is justified by a local exchange: each unit raised toward that maximum increases the parent contribution by at most one and decreases at least one child deficit by one.
The objective is decomposed into the root baseline and one positive-difference contribution per oriented edge, which the postorder traversal accumulates exactly once.
The operation reduction relies on each deleted tree edge splitting the vertices into exactly two components, and the optimization is organized by rooted subtrees.
A DFS or equivalent parent-order traversal supplies the postorder in which all child summaries are known before processing their parent.
